title: Today's AI Brief: Māori Text-to-Speech Model Spurns Big Tech’s Values
summary: A Māori text-to-speech model challenges big tech, Groq raises $650M, and more AI updates.

imageCaption: Māori Text-to-Speech Model Spurns Big Tech’s Values
imageSourceTitle: Māori Text-to-Speech Model Spurns Big Tech’s Values

body.text: I'm Leo Stone, and I'm excited to share some fresh AI news with you. Today, we're looking at a fascinating text-to-speech model from New Zealand that's making waves, and a big funding round for an AI chip startup. It's always great to see innovative developments in the AI space, and these stories caught my attention.

First up, a Māori text-to-speech model is challenging big tech's values. This model, developed in New Zealand, aims to preserve the Māori language and culture. What's interesting here is that the team behind it is prioritizing cultural sensitivity and community involvement over the typical big tech approach. It's a refreshing shift in the AI landscape, and it's great to see a focus on preserving indigenous cultures.

Next, AI chip startup Groq is reportedly raising $650M in funding. This comes after Nvidia's $20B 'not-acqui-hire' deal, which is a fancy way of saying they didn't quite acquire them, but still got a sweet deal. Groq's chips are designed for AI workloads, and this funding round shows investors are eager to support innovative AI infrastructure. It's exciting to see the growth of AI-specific hardware.

Māori Text-to-Speech Model Spurns Big Tech’s Values is also worth a quick mention. The main content says: New Zealand is a country famed for its dramatic landscapes, but its linguistic landscape is arguably just as interesting. Of its three official languages, only te reo Māori (the Māori language) could be described as indigenous. Though spoken fluently by just 4.3 percent of the population, national statistics show that about 30 percent of New Zealanders can ... The point is not to force it into a giant theory; it is simply part of today's news mix.

The point is not to force it into a giant theory; it is simply part of today's news mix.

My quick read is that today's set of stories is useful because it gives us texture, not a sermon. Each item has its own little job: one tells us what changed, another tells us what people are worried about, and another gives the day its mood.
